text: The e-reading versus print debate is less about technology than about environmental cues. Researcher Anne Mangen found that readers of print texts significantly outperformed e-reader users on narrative reconstruction — they remembered plot events in correct sequence and with greater detail. Her explanation was spatial: a physical book provides a proprioceptive map of the text. Readers know approximately where in the body of the book a passage lives. This tactile orientation, absent in scrollable interfaces, appears to anchor memory encoding. The book's weight and thickness are not ornamental features; they are part of the cognitive apparatus of deep reading.
question: How did Anne Mangen explain why print readers outperformed e-reader users at narrative reconstruction?

options.text: Print text uses fonts optimized for the visual cortex.
Print readers tend to read more slowly as a learned habit.
Physical books provide a proprioceptive spatial map that anchors memory encoding.
E-readers produce blue light that impairs long-term memory consolidation.
